summary: The author discusses pedagogical challenges when teaching TLA+ through the PlusCal abstraction layer. While PlusCal simplifies syntax significantly, certain advanced concepts like temporal invariants require underlying TLA+ knowledge that isn't otherwise needed in PlusCal. Rather than either using clunky workarounds or dumping inapplicable concepts on readers, the author explores a third approach: finding ways to make each prerequisite concept independently useful within PlusCal contexts, then synthesizing them later when needed.
key_points: PlusCal abstracts away TLA+'s most convoluted syntax while preserving most of the learning, making it ideal for teaching
Some invariants (like 'x never decreases') are trivial in pure TLA+ but require ugly bookkeeping hacks in PlusCal
The pedagogical dilemma: teach concepts that aren't immediately useful, or use inferior workarounds
The solution is to find natural contexts where each prerequisite concept becomes useful on its own, enabling later synthesis
Stuttering can be taught through crash simulation, primed variables through debugging error traces
money_quotes: I asked my technical reviewer to be 'almost-comically brutal' and he's risen to the challenge.
PlusCal only abstracts away setting and updating values, which happens to be the most convoluted part of TLA+.
I don't want to sledgehammer the reader with information that only makes sense much later.
This works, but it has two major problems... We've added another variable to track solely to help with the bookkeeping.
We make things cumbersome or we could write `[][y => x' >= x]_x`. No bookkeeping needed at all.
Either we use a shitty workaround or we bite the bullet and teach a bunch of inapplicable concepts to get to the good stuff.
While all these concepts are unnecessary in Pluscal, I can find ways to make them useful.
key_insight: When teaching abstractions that leak, the solution isn't to dump prerequisites or accept inferior workarounds, but to find independent contexts where each prerequisite becomes naturally useful before synthesis is needed.
